title: Hormonal Rage: Estrogen, Progesterone, Testosterone
dek: Your blood is singing a very old, very loud song. When the hormones shift, the rage that rises isn't a mistake or a malfunction - it is the body demanding that you finally pay attention to what you have been swallowing for weeks.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Heat in the Blood


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.219Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the sheets are damp against your lower back. There is a specific kind of heat that blooms in the center of your chest, right behind the sternum, radiating outward until it hits the inner architecture of your ribs. It is not the heat of a summer afternoon. It is the heat of a furnace door swinging open. Your jaw is already locked, the masseter muscles tight as coiled wire, grinding down on the molars as if you could chew through the dark itself. This is the hour of the liver, according to Traditional Chinese Medicine, the time when the body processes anger and frustration. And right now, your liver is shouting.

You might have been told that this is just "hormones." You might have been handed a pamphlet about PMS or perimenopause, told to take a deep breath, maybe offered a prescription to smooth out the edges. But the body does not create this kind of fire by accident. The sudden drop in estrogen, the erratic spikes of progesterone, the quiet surge of testosterone - these are not glitches in the system. They are amplifiers. They take the quiet, polite frustrations you have been swallowing all month and turn them into a roar. 

When estrogen plummets, the serotonin in your brain drops with it. The buffer is gone. The padding that allowed you to smile when your boss interrupted you, or when your partner left the dishes in the sink again, has evaporated. What is left is the raw, unvarnished truth of your boundaries. The rage you feel is not a pathology; it is biology doing its job. It is the body saying, *No more.*

## The Architecture of the Surge

Feel the back of your neck right now. Bring your hand up and press your fingers into the levator scapulae, the muscles that run from the top of your cervical spine down to your shoulder blades. If you are in the middle of a hormonal shift, these muscles are likely hard as stone. They are preparing you to fight. They are pulling your shoulders up to protect your throat. This is an ancient, mammalian response. 

Bessel van der Kolk, in his groundbreaking work on trauma and the body, reminds us that the body keeps the score. But it doesn't just keep the score of major traumas; it keeps the score of every micro-aggression, every swallowed "no," every time you made yourself smaller to make someone else comfortable. When the hormonal tide goes out, all those rocks are exposed on the beach. The anger you feel is the accumulated weight of all those moments, suddenly demanding to be felt.

You might notice that the rage feels different depending on where you are in your cycle or your life. The anger of high testosterone - which can happen when estrogen drops - is sharp, outward, and decisive. It wants to break things. It wants to snap the pencil in half. The anger of low progesterone is more like a weeping wound, a profound irritability that makes the sound of someone chewing across the room feel like a physical assault. 

## The Wisdom of the Fire

We are taught that anger is ugly, especially in women. We are taught that it makes us unlovable, dangerous, or crazy. But Karla McLaren, a brilliant researcher of emotions, teaches that anger is the emotion of boundaries. It is the honorable sentry that stands at the edge of your soul and says, *What is mine is mine, and what is yours is yours.* When your hormones shift and the sentry suddenly gets very loud, it is not because the sentry is broken. It is because the sentry has finally been given a megaphone.

Think about the last time you felt this hormonal rage. Where did it live in your body? Did it sit in the psoas, deep in the bowl of your pelvis, making you want to kick or run? Did it tighten your diaphragm, making your breath shallow and fast? The body knows exactly what it is doing. It is mobilizing energy to protect you. 

Instead of trying to meditate the anger away, or shame yourself for feeling it, what if you listened to it? What if you sat on the floor, felt the solid ground beneath your sit bones, and asked the fire what it needs to burn? 

## Herbal Allies for the Sentry

The earth provides medicine for this fire, not to extinguish it, but to help the body hold it without burning down the house. We do not use herbs to silence the body; we use them to support the nervous system so that the message can be delivered clearly.

Motherwort (*Leonurus cardiaca*) is the great mother of the heart. When the chest is tight, when the palpitations start, when the rage feels like it might tear your heart muscle in two, Motherwort steps in. It is intensely bitter, and that bitterness is exactly what the liver needs to move the stagnant energy. A few drops of [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) on the tongue can bring the energy down from the head and back into the center of the body.

Vitex, or Chaste Tree Berry, is another profound ally. It does not contain hormones itself, but it speaks directly to the pituitary gland, the master conductor of the endocrine orchestra. It helps the body find its own rhythm again. When the progesterone is too low and the estrogen is dominating, creating that weeping, irritable rage, [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help the body remember how to produce its own soothing progesterone.

And then there is Cramp Bark (*Viburnum opulus*). While it is famous for easing uterine cramping, it is also a profound skeletal muscle relaxant. When the jaw is locked, when the trapezius muscles are screaming, [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps the physical tension release so that the emotional tension can move through.

## The Practice of Grounding the Charge

When the surge hits, you need a physical practice to move the energy. You cannot think your way out of a somatic experience. 

**The Practice: The Heel Drop**
Stand up. Take off your shoes. Feel the floor beneath the soles of your feet. Notice the texture - is it wood, tile, carpet? Now, lift your heels off the ground, coming up onto the balls of your feet. Hold for a second, and then drop your heels hard into the floor. Let the impact travel up your legs, through your pelvis, and up your spine. Do it again. Lift, and drop. Let your jaw hang slack. Let your shoulders drop with the impact. 

As you drop your heels, make a sound. It doesn't have to be a scream; it can be a low, guttural grunt. *Huh.* Let the sound come from the belly, not the throat. Do this ten times. Feel the vibration in your bones. This is how you tell the nervous system that the energy has a place to go. You are grounding the lightning rod.

## The Courage to Stay

Gabor Maté writes extensively about how the suppression of emotion, particularly anger, leads to chronic illness. When we swallow the fire, it burns us from the inside out. It turns into autoimmune conditions, chronic pain, and deep, systemic inflammation. The hormonal shifts of our cycles, or the massive transition of perimenopause, are the body's refusal to keep swallowing the fire.

It takes immense courage to stay with the physical sensation of rage without acting out and without shutting down. It means feeling the heat in the face, the trembling in the hands, the absolute certainty that you are right and everyone else is wrong, and just breathing into the center of it. 

You do not have to fix the hormones. You have to listen to what they are amplifying. The rage is a messenger. It is telling you where your life is too small, where your boundaries are too porous, where you have given away too much of your power. 

## The Integration of the Shadow

We are so afraid of our own darkness. We are afraid that if we let the anger out, it will destroy everything we love. But Thich Nhat Hanh, the great Zen master, taught that we must treat our anger like a crying baby. We do not throw the baby out; we hold it tenderly. We say, *Breathing in, I know that anger is in me. Breathing out, I am taking good care of my anger.*

This is the ultimate somatic work. To hold the fire with tenderness. To feel the testosterone surging, making your muscles twitch with the desire to fight, and to say to your body, *I feel you. I am listening.* To feel the estrogen dropping, leaving you raw and exposed, and to say, *I will protect you.*

Your body is not betraying you. It is finally telling you the truth.

## FAQ

**Q: Is it normal to feel this angry right before my period?**
**A:** Yes. The drop in estrogen and progesterone right before menstruation removes the neurochemical buffers that help you tolerate stress. The anger you feel is real; the hormones just removed the filter. It is your body's way of highlighting what is not working in your life.

**Q: Should I get my hormones tested if the rage is unbearable?**
**A:** Testing can be incredibly validating. It can show you the exact landscape of your endocrine system. But remember that the numbers on a page are just a map; the territory is your body. Use the data to support yourself, but do not use it to pathologize your emotions.

**Q: How do I know if my anger is hormonal or if I actually need to change my life?**
**A:** It is almost always both. The hormones do not invent the anger; they amplify what is already there. If you are furious at your partner every month before your period, the issue isn't just the hormones - it's the dynamic in the relationship that you are able to ignore the rest of the month.

**Q: Can herbs really help with this level of intensity?**
**A:** Herbs are not suppressants like pharmaceuticals. They will not numb you out. But they can profoundly nourish the nervous system and the liver, helping your body process the hormones more efficiently and giving your nervous system the resilience to hold the emotional charge without shattering.

**Q: What if I accidentally lash out when the rage hits?**
**A:** You are human. When the nervous system is flooded, the prefrontal cortex goes offline. If you lash out, repair the rupture. Apologize for the behavior, but do not apologize for the feeling. Learn the somatic signs of the rising tide - the tight jaw, the shallow breath - so you can step away before the wave breaks next time.

## The Anatomy of the Swell

Let us look closer at the physical reality of this hormonal tide. When you are in the luteal phase of your cycle, or when you are navigating the chaotic waters of perimenopause, the body is doing an immense amount of metabolic work. The liver, which is responsible for clearing excess hormones from the bloodstream, is working overtime. If your liver is sluggish - perhaps from stress, environmental toxins, or simply the sheer volume of emotional processing you are doing - the hormones back up in the system. 

This backup is not just a biochemical event; it is a somatic one. You can feel it in the right side of your abdomen, just under the ribcage. It might feel like a dull ache, a sense of fullness, or a subtle heat. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the liver is the general of the army. It is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi, or life force, throughout the body. When the liver is congested, the Qi stagnates. And stagnant Qi, especially liver Qi, manifests as frustration, irritability, and explosive rage.

This is why the anger feels so physical. It is not in your head; it is in your organs. It is in the fascia that wraps around your liver, pulling tight and restricting movement. When you feel that sudden spike of fury because someone left their shoes in the hallway, you are not just reacting to the shoes. You are reacting to the stagnant energy in your own abdomen, crying out for movement.

## The Role of the Psoas

We cannot talk about hormonal rage without talking about the psoas muscle. The psoas is the deepest muscle in the human body, connecting the lumbar spine to the femur. It is the only muscle that connects the upper body to the lower body. It is also the muscle of the fight-or-flight response. When you are startled, the psoas contracts, pulling you into a fetal position to protect your vital organs.

During hormonal shifts, the nervous system is highly sensitized. The threshold for what constitutes a "threat" is lowered. A loud noise, a demanding email, a sudden change in plans - all of these can trigger the psoas to contract. And because the psoas is intimately connected to the diaphragm through a web of fascia, when the psoas tightens, your breathing becomes shallow. 

Shallow breathing signals to the brain that you are in danger, which triggers the release of adrenaline and cortisol, which further sensitizes the nervous system. It is a feedback loop of tension and fear that quickly transmutes into rage. The anger is the body's attempt to fight off the perceived threat. 

To break this loop, you have to speak to the psoas. You have to lie on the floor, put your legs up on a chair, and let the lower back completely release into the ground. You have to breathe deep into the belly, expanding the diaphragm and gently stretching the psoas. You have to tell the body, in its own language of pressure and release, that it is safe.

## The Cultural Conditioning of the Good Girl

Why is this hormonal rage so terrifying to us? Why do we rush to medicate it, suppress it, or apologize for it? Because we have been conditioned, from the moment we were born, to be the "Good Girl." The Good Girl is compliant. She is quiet. She is accommodating. She does not take up too much space, and she certainly does not burn the house down.

Resmaa Menakem, in his profound work on somatic abolitionism, teaches that trauma is decontextualized in the body over time. The trauma of patriarchy, the trauma of having to suppress our life force to survive, lives in our tissues. It lives in the tight jaw, the constricted throat, the shallow breath. 

When the hormones shift and the neurochemical buffers are removed, the Good Girl conditioning begins to crack. The raw, untamed life force underneath - the wild woman, the hag, the witch - starts to claw her way to the surface. The rage you feel is the friction between the conditioning that says "stay small" and the biology that says "expand."

It is terrifying because it threatens the structures of your life. If you let the anger speak, you might have to quit the job. You might have to leave the relationship. You might have to set a boundary that will make someone else very angry. The hormonal rage is a truth serum, and the truth is often highly inconvenient.

## The Medicine of the Bitter

In Western herbalism, we rely heavily on bitter herbs to support the liver and move stagnant energy. We have lost our taste for bitterness in the modern world. We want everything sweet, palatable, and easy. But the body needs the bitter. The bitter taste on the tongue stimulates the vagus nerve, which sends a signal to the digestive system to release bile, enzymes, and gastric juices. It literally gets things moving.

When the hormonal rage is stuck in your chest and your jaw, you need the bitter. Dandelion root (*Taraxacum officinale*) is a profound ally here. It is a humble weed, but it is a powerhouse for the liver. It helps the liver clear the excess hormones and move the stagnant Qi. Drinking a strong, bitter cup of Dandelion root tea is like opening the windows in a stuffy room. It lets the air circulate.

We must learn to tolerate the bitter, both in our medicine and in our emotional lives. The anger is bitter. The grief that often sits underneath the anger is bitter. But if we can swallow it, if we can digest it, it becomes medicine. It becomes the fuel for our own liberation.

## The Somatics of the Jaw

Let us return to the jaw. The masseter muscle, which closes the jaw, is one of the strongest muscles in the body relative to its size. It is also a primary holding site for unexpressed anger. When you want to scream, but you force yourself to smile, the energy gets trapped in the masseter. 

Over years and decades, this chronic tension can lead to TMJ, headaches, and a constant, low-grade sense of frustration. During hormonal shifts, when the body is already primed for fight-or-flight, the jaw can become agonizingly tight. 

You can release this tension manually. Take your thumbs and press them into the soft tissue just under your cheekbones, right where the upper and lower jaw meet. Press firmly and hold. It will likely be exquisitely tender. Breathe into the pain. As you hold the pressure, slowly open and close your mouth. Let the jaw track slightly side to side. You are physically breaking up the adhesions in the fascia. You are giving the trapped energy a way out.

As you do this, you might find that emotion rises. You might suddenly start to cry, or you might feel a wave of heat wash over your face. Let it happen. The body is unwinding the story it has been holding.

## The Descent into the Dark

Perimenopause, in particular, is a profound descent into the dark. It is the autumn of a woman's life, the time when the leaves fall and the bare branches are exposed. The erratic fluctuations of estrogen and progesterone during this time can create a landscape of emotional chaos. One day you are weeping over a commercial; the next day you are ready to burn down the grocery store because they are out of your favorite tea.

This is not a disease. It is an initiation. 

In many indigenous cultures, the cessation of the menstrual cycle is seen as a time of immense power. The woman is no longer bleeding out her life force every month; she is retaining it. She is becoming a wise elder. But the transition from the mother to the crone is not a peaceful one. It is a death and a rebirth. 

The rage of perimenopause is the fire that burns away the false self. It burns away the need to please, the need to accommodate, the need to be the Good Girl. It is a holy fire. 

Pema Chödrön teaches that we must lean into the sharp points of our experience. We must not run away from the pain, the anger, or the fear. We must sit right in the middle of the fire and let it burn away everything that is not true. 

When the hormonal rage hits, do not run. Do not immediately reach for the phone to distract yourself. Do not immediately try to fix it. Sit in the fire. Feel the heat in your blood. Feel the tension in your muscles. Ask the fire what it is trying to tell you. 

## The Alchemy of the Breath

The breath is the bridge between the conscious and the unconscious mind. It is the only autonomic function that we can also consciously control. When the hormonal rage is surging, the breath becomes shallow and erratic. The sympathetic nervous system is in overdrive. 

To shift the nervous system, we must change the breath. We must use the breath to signal to the body that we are safe, even in the midst of the fire. 

**The Practice: The Lengthened Exhale**
Sit comfortably, or lie on your back. Place one hand on your belly and one hand on your chest. Breathe in through your nose for a count of four, feeling the belly expand into your hand. The chest should remain relatively still. Now, purse your lips as if you are blowing through a straw, and exhale slowly for a count of eight. 

The lengthened exhale stimulates the vagus nerve, which activates the parasympathetic nervous system - the rest and digest state. It lowers the heart rate, reduces blood pressure, and tells the amygdala to stand down. 

Do this for five minutes. Notice how the physical sensation of the anger shifts. It may not go away completely, but the sharp, jagged edges will begin to soften. The fire will become a steady, manageable flame.

## The Integration of the Whole

We are not trying to become perfectly calm, serene beings who never feel anger. That is a false, disembodied ideal. We are trying to become whole. We are trying to integrate all the parts of ourselves - the light and the dark, the calm and the storm, the estrogen and the testosterone.

When we welcome the hormonal rage as a messenger, rather than rejecting it as a malfunction, we reclaim our power. We stop fighting our own biology. We learn to ride the waves of our hormones with grace and fierce self-compassion.

The next time the heat rises in your chest, the next time your jaw locks and your blood sings that old, loud song, remember this: You are not broken. You are alive. And your body is fighting for you.

## The Cellular Memory of the Fascia

We must delve deeper into the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps every muscle, bone, nerve, and organ in the body. The fascia is not just structural wrapping; it is a highly innervated, communicative web. It is the physical substrate of the unconscious mind. When you experience an emotion that you cannot fully process or express - like the anger you swallowed when you were dismissed in a meeting, or the frustration you pushed down when your boundaries were crossed - the fascia tightens around that unexpressed energy. It creates a physical holding pattern.

Over the course of a menstrual cycle, as hormones ebb and flow, the water content and the elasticity of the fascia change. Estrogen helps keep the fascia hydrated and supple. When estrogen drops, the fascia becomes stiffer, more rigid. Those holding patterns, those pockets of trapped anger, suddenly become much more pronounced. The physical stiffness you feel in your joints and muscles right before your period is not just fluid retention; it is the fascia tightening around the stories you haven't told.

This is why somatic movement is so critical. You cannot stretch the fascia the way you stretch a muscle. You have to melt it. You have to use slow, sustained pressure and mindful movement to coax the tissue into releasing. When you do this, you are not just releasing physical tension; you are releasing the trapped emotional charge. You are letting the anger out of the tissue.

## The Symphony of the Endocrine System

The endocrine system is a symphony, and the hormones are the instruments. Estrogen is the strings - fluid, expansive, and connective. Progesterone is the woodwinds - soothing, grounding, and rhythmic. Testosterone is the brass - sharp, decisive, and driving. When the symphony is in balance, the music is beautiful. But during hormonal transitions, the conductor steps off the podium. The strings snap, the woodwinds go flat, and the brass section starts blaring.

This cacophony is what we experience as hormonal rage. It is the sound of the system trying to find a new equilibrium. And just like a symphony, you cannot fix the cacophony by silencing one section. You have to bring the whole orchestra back into harmony. 

This requires deep, systemic nourishment. It requires feeding the body the building blocks it needs to create hormones - healthy fats, complex carbohydrates, and high-quality proteins. It requires resting when the body asks for rest, rather than pushing through with caffeine and adrenaline. It requires treating the body with the reverence it deserves, rather than treating it like a machine that is breaking down.

## The Sacred Rage

Ultimately, we must reframe our relationship with anger. We must stop seeing it as a toxic emotion that needs to be managed, and start seeing it as a sacred force that needs to be channeled. Sacred rage is the energy that says "no" to injustice, "no" to violation, "no" to the diminishment of the soul. It is the energy that protects the vulnerable and demands integrity.

When your hormones amplify this rage, they are giving you a gift. They are giving you access to the raw power of your own life force. They are asking you to step into your authority. 

Do not waste this energy by turning it inward against yourself in the form of shame or self-criticism. Do not waste it by lashing out at the people around you in petty arguments. Channel it. Use it to fuel your creative work. Use it to set the boundaries you have been afraid to set. Use it to dismantle the structures in your life that are no longer serving you.

The fire is yours. Let it burn clean. Let it illuminate the path forward. Let it remind you, in every cell of your body, that you are a force of nature, and you will not be silenced.
